diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Abrupt fever and rigors are characteristic of the systemic response to streptococcal erysipelas and mark systemic toxicity.
Contact dermatitis does not cause high fever with rigors.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fiery-red, sharply demarcated, raised facial plaque with a firm advancing border

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sharply demarcated, raised, fiery-red plaque distinguishes erysipelas from deeper cellulitis and from flat inflammatory rashes.
Facial infection near the orbit raises the possibility of orbital extension — an emergency that cannot be excluded remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Systemic toxicity — diaphoresis, prostration, tachycardia by wearable (~118)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Systemic toxicity upgrades a skin infection from home-manageable to needing urgent in-person (often intravenous) treatment; patient-obtainable so admissible, unverified.

symptoms.symptom_name: Patient-reportable orbital screen negative (no eye pain on movement, no diplopia, vision intact)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Reassuring but not exclusionary — extraocular movements, proptosis and acuity require an in-person exam the AI Doctor cannot perform.

symptoms.symptom_name: Peau d'orange lymphatic oedema and tender submandibular/preauricular lymphadenopathy

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Superficial lymphatic congestion and regional nodes support erysipelas — sealed clinician palpation findings, not obtainable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Extraocular movements intact, no proptosis, visual acuity unaffected, no bullae or crepitus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: These clinician-measured signs exclude orbital and necrotising involvement — precisely the exam the AI Doctor cannot do, which is why escalation to in-person assessment is mandatory.
